q1_pregnant_or_postpartum.text: Is the patient currently pregnant, or has she given birth within the last 6 weeks?

q2_active_bleeding.text: Is there any bleeding right now â€” from the vagina, or any other bleeding that may be pregnancy-related?

q3_heavy_bleeding.text: Is the bleeding heavy â€” for example, soaking through a pad quickly, pooling on the floor, or dripping?

q4_seizure.text: Has the patient had a seizure, convulsion, or fit â€” shaking uncontrollably or losing consciousness suddenly?
q4_seizure.guidance_note: Seizures in pregnancy after 20 weeks are eclampsia until proven otherwise â€” a life-threatening emergency. Do not restrain the patient during a seizure. Protect from injury and time the seizure.

q5_cord_prolapse.text: Has anyone mentioned seeing the cord coming out before the baby, or is there something visible at the birth canal that is not the baby's head?

q6_labour_stage.text: Is the baby's head visible or about to be born, or is the patient still in early labour?

q7_complications.text: Are there any complications â€” bleeding, the patient feeling faint or dizzy, the baby not moving normally, or the membranes have been ruptured for more than 18 hours?

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
This is an obstetric emergency â€” help is on the way.
If the patient is lying down, help her lie flat on her back with her legs slightly elevated.
Do not try to deliver the baby or remove anything from the birth canal.
If there is bleeding, place a clean pad or cloth against the bleeding area.
If the patient feels faint or her skin turns pale and clammy, help her lie flat and elevate her legs slightly.
Monitor her consciousness â€” if she becomes less responsive, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
A seizure in pregnancy is a life-threatening emergency â€” help is on the way.
Do not try to restrain her or hold her down during a seizure.
Clear the area around her of hard or sharp objects to prevent injury.
After the seizure, gently turn her onto her left side â€” this protects her airway.
Do not put anything in her mouth.
Time how long the seizure lasts and tell me.
If another seizure starts, protect her from injury and time it.
Stay on the line with me.
This is a cord prolapse â€” a life-threatening emergency for the baby.
Ask the patient to kneel on the bed with her head and chest on pillows, bottom in the air â€” this is called the knee-chest position.
This position takes pressure off the cord and protects the baby's blood supply.
Do not push the cord back in, and do not try to deliver the baby.
Keep her in this position until the ambulance crew arrives.
Stay on the line with me.
There are complications during this delivery â€” help is on the way.
Help the patient lie on her left side.
Do not try to deliver the baby or remove anything.
If bleeding starts, apply a clean cloth with gentle pressure.
Keep her warm and reassure her.
Monitor her consciousness and tell me if anything changes.
Stay on the line with me.
An ambulance is on the way.
Help the patient lie on her left side for comfort.
If the baby is about to be born and no one can stop it, support the baby's head as it comes out â€” do not pull on it.
Wrap the baby in clean, warm clothes immediately after birth.
Do not cut the cord unless you are trained to do so.
Keep the baby skin-to-skin with the mother to keep warm.
This call does not appear to be an obstetric emergency based on the answers given.
Reassess the chief complaint and select the appropriate protocol.
